pi_items.source: Task instructions explicitly specify saving the final output as '/home/ga/Documents/SAM_Projects/12x24_profile.json'.
Task instructions explicitly state 'simulate a 50 MW (50,000 kW DC) system'.

Python simulation using PySAM's Pvwattsv8 with the task parameters yields an annual energy of ~103,665,294 to ~106,052,547 kWh depending on defaults, validating 90,000,000 as a correct and robust lower bound.

Python simulation using PySAM's Pvwattsv8 with the task parameters yields an annual energy of ~103,665,294 to ~106,052,547 kWh depending on defaults, validating 125,000,000 as a correct and robust upper bound.
Python simulation using PySAM's Pvwattsv8 computes a June noon peak generation of ~34,626 kW, verifying that a 20,000 kW minimum is physically sound for a 50 MW DC tracking system.

privileged_info_summary: The task relies on a real-world Typical Meteorological Year (TMY) weather file for Phoenix, AZ, sourced from NREL. By running the PySAM Pvwattsv8 simulation with the specified 50 MW DC system parameters (1.2 DC/AC ratio, 96% inverter efficiency, 1-axis tracking, 14% losses), the actual calculated annual energy is approximately 103.6 GWh to 106.0 GWh depending on implicit defaults. This fully validates the minimum (90,000,000 kWh) and maximum (125,000,000 kWh) energy bounds, as well as the 20,000 kW minimum threshold for peak hour generation (which empirically hits ~34,626 kW in June at noon).
